Easy.q: Who was the leader of Nazi Germany during World War II?
Easy.hint: He became Chancellor of Germany in 1933.

Easy.q: Which country did Germany invade on September 1, 1939, triggering the start of World War II?
Easy.hint: This country borders Germany to the east.

Easy.q: What wall was built in 1961 to divide the capital city of Germany during the Cold War?
Easy.hint: It separated the communist East from the capitalist West.

Medium.q: What was the name of the German democratic government established after World War I?
Medium.hint: It was named after the town where its constitution was drawn up.

Medium.q: Who discovered the first miracle antibiotic, Penicillin, in 1928?
Medium.hint: He noticed a mysterious mold growing on a petri dish of bacteria.

Medium.q: Which US President was in office during the Cuban Missile Crisis of 1962?
Medium.hint: He was famously assassinated in Dallas, Texas in 1963.

Medium.q: What was the name of the international peacekeeping organization created immediately after World War I?
Medium.hint: It ultimately failed to prevent the rise of fascism in the 1930s.

Medium.q: What name was given to the secret operations to rescue Allied soldiers from the beaches of France in 1940?
Medium.hint: Hundreds of civilian 'little ships' sailed across the English Channel to help.

Hard.q: What was the name of the law passed in 1933 that gave Adolf Hitler dictatorial powers to make laws without parliament?
Hard.hint: It effectively legally ended democracy in the Weimar Republic.

Hard.q: Who developed the 'Germ Theory of Disease' in 1861, proving that microbes caused sickness?
Hard.hint: He was a French chemist who originally studied why beer and wine went sour.

Hard.q: What was the name of the military alliance formed by the Soviet Union and its Eastern European satellite states in 1955?
Hard.hint: It was created as a direct counterweight to the West's NATO alliance.

Hard.q: Which British physician discovered the vaccine for smallpox in 1796 using cowpox fluid?
Hard.hint: He tested his theory on a young boy named James Phipps.

Hard.q: What 1929 economic disaster triggered mass unemployment in Germany and helped the Nazi party gain votes?
Hard.hint: It began with a massive stock market collapse in New York.

Hard.q: In which city was an atomic bomb dropped by the United States on August 6, 1945?
Hard.hint: It was followed three days later by a second bomb dropped on Nagasaki.

Hard.q: What was the main purpose of the Nuremberg Laws passed by the Nazis in 1935?
options: To strip Jewish citizens of their German citizenship and civil rights
Hard.hint: These laws focused heavily on racial segregation and definition.

Hard.q: Who mapped out the Broad Street cholera outbreak in London in 1854, proving it was waterborne?
Hard.hint: He famously removed the handle from the contaminated water pump.

Challenger.q: What was the name of the 1924 plan that restructured Germany's WWI reparations payments and secured American loans?
Challenger.hint: It helped launch the stable 'Golden Age' of the Weimar Republic.

Challenger.q: Who published a breakthrough book on human anatomy in 1543 based on his own human dissections?
Challenger.hint: His famous anatomical masterpiece was titled 'On the Fabric of the Human Body'.

Challenger.q: What did the US Truman Doctrine of 1947 explicitly pledge to do?
options: Contain the global spread of communism by supporting vulnerable nations
Challenger.hint: It shifted American foreign policy toward active containment.

Challenger.q: What 1934 event involved Hitler purging and executing the leadership of the SA (Brownshirts), including Ernst Röhm?
options: The Night of the Long Knives
Challenger.hint: It neutralized internal party opposition and secured the loyalty of the army.

Challenger.q: Who proved that blood circulates continuously around the human body in his 1628 publication?
Challenger.hint: He discovered that the heart acts directly as a mechanical pump.

Challenger.q: What agreement was signed in 1938 allowing Germany to annex the Sudetenland region of Czechoslovakia?
Challenger.hint: Neville Chamberlain infamously declared it brought 'peace for our time'.

Challenger.q: What triggered the Berlin Blockade by the Soviet Union in 1948?
options: The construction of the Berlin Wall
The introduction of the new Deutschmark currency by Western allies
An invasion attempt by the USA
Challenger.hint: Stalin attempted to starve the Western zones of Berlin into submission by shutting land routes.

Challenger.q: Who wrote 'The Report on the Sanitary Conditions of the Labouring Population' in 1842, sparking public health reform?
Challenger.hint: He argued that poor sanitation caused disease, which cost the taxpayer money.

Challenger.q: What was the name of the secret treaty signed between Nazi Germany and the Soviet Union in August 1939?
Challenger.hint: It included a secret clause to divide Poland between them.

Challenger.q: Which ancient medical theory argued that disease was caused by an imbalance of blood, phlegm, black bile, and yellow bile?
Challenger.hint: It was created by Hippocrates and expanded significantly by Galen.

Challenger.q: What was the primary goal of the 1968 'Prague Spring' uprising in Czechoslovakia?
options: To exit the Warsaw Pact immediately
To introduce 'socialism with a human face' through political liberalization
To launch an attack on West Germany
Challenger.hint: Led by Alexander Dubček, it was crushed by Soviet tanks.

Challenger.q: What title did Adolf Hitler assume in August 1934 following the death of President Hindenburg?
Challenger.hint: It combined the roles of both Chancellor and President into one absolute leader.

Challenger.q: Who introduced carbolic acid spray in 1865 to dramatically reduce infections during surgical procedures?
Challenger.hint: His work pioneered antiseptic surgery techniques.

Challenger.q: What strategy did the USA use during the Vietnam War that involved destroying jungle foliage to expose enemy hiding spots?
options: The use of chemical defoliants like Agent Orange
Challenger.hint: It killed plant life on a massive scale, causing long-term toxic issues.

Challenger.q: What name was given to the economic crisis in Germany in 1923 when the government printed money to pay striking workers in the Ruhr?
Challenger.hint: Prices rose so fast that money became completely worthless, and people burned banknotes for heat.

Easy.q: Which article of the Weimar Constitution gave the President emergency powers to rule by decree without parliament?
Easy.hint: It was intended for crises but was frequently used by Hindenburg and later Hitler.

Easy.q: What name is given to the ideological division of Europe into separate spheres of influence after World War II?
Easy.hint: Winston Churchill coined this famous phrase in a 1946 speech in Fulton, Missouri.

Easy.q: Who developed the first effective chemical antiseptic to prevent infection in hospital operating theatres?
Easy.hint: He realized that carbolic acid used on sewage could clean open wounds.

Medium.q: What was the historical significance of the 1925 Locarno Treaties for Germany?
options: Germany lost more territory to France
Germany accepted its western borders and was allowed to join the League of Nations
Germany signed a secret alliance with Russia
Germany refused to pay any more reparations
Medium.hint: It was a major diplomatic success negotiated by Gustav Stresemann.

Medium.q: How did Robert Koch improve upon Louis Pasteur's bacteriology discoveries?
options: He identified and isolated the specific microbes responsible for anthrax, tuberculosis, and cholera
He disproved the Four Humours theory
Medium.hint: He created an exact scientific method of staining and photographing individual bacteria types.

Medium.q: What was the purpose of the 1947 American Marshall Plan?
options: To build nuclear missile silos in Europe
To provide over $13 billion in economic aid to help rebuild Western Europe and resist communism
Medium.hint: The USA believed that prosperous, stable nations would never turn to communism.

Medium.q: What political arrangement did the 1919 Treaty of Versailles force Germany to accept regarding the Rhineland?
options: It was completely annexed by France
It was permanently demilitarized to act as a buffer zone
It was handed over to the League of Nations
Medium.hint: German troops were strictly forbidden from entering this western border zone.

Medium.q: What was the direct impact of the 1875 Public Health Act in Great Britain?
options: It made working in factories safer for children
It forced local authorities to provide clean water, sewers, and street lighting
It introduced the National Health Service (NHS)
It banned the sale of traditional herbal medicine
Medium.hint: It marked a definitive shift away from the government's traditional 'laissez-faire' (leave alone) attitude.

Hard.q: What internal conflict occurred within the Nazi Party during the June 1934 'Night of the Long Knives'?
options: Hitler eliminated the socialist-leaning leadership of the SA paramilitary to secure the backing of the regular army
The German army assassinated Ernst Röhm
The Communist Party attempted an armed uprising
Hard.hint: The regular army feared the SA's desire to merge with or replace them.

Hard.q: What was the critical significance of Paul Ehrlich's 1909 discovery of Salvarsan 606?
options: It was the first effective vaccine
It was the first 'magic bullet'—a chemical compound that targeted a specific disease without harming the body
It proved that germs caused rot
Hard.hint: It was designed to cure syphilis and paved the way for modern chemotherapy.

Hard.q: What did the Soviet Union's Brezhnev Doctrine of 1968 assert?
options: The USSR would trade freely with America
The USSR reserved the right to intervene militarily in any Eastern Bloc country where socialism was threatened
The USSR would dismantle all its nuclear weapons
The USSR would build a wall across Czechoslovakia
Hard.hint: It was used to retroactively justify the brutal invasion of Czechoslovakia.

Hard.q: What structural flaw in the Weimar voting system made it incredibly difficult to form a strong, stable majority government?
options: The total ban on small political parties
The exclusion of women from voting
Hard.hint: It resulted in dozens of small parties winning seats, forcing weak coalition governments.

Hard.q: What medical advancement did the work of Howard Florey and Ernst Chain achieve during World War II?
options: They successfully purified and mass-produced Penicillin for wartime casualties
Hard.hint: They took Alexander Fleming's forgotten 1928 observation and turned it into a usable drug.

Hard.q: What was the purpose of the 1975 Helsinki Accords during the Cold War détente era?
options: To ban all intercontinental ballistic missiles
To recognize European post-WWII borders and commit to improving human rights
To unify East and West Germany
To dissolve the Warsaw Pact alliance
Hard.hint: It was a major effort to reduce tension between Western and Eastern Blocs.

Hard.q: How did the 1933 Reichstag Fire benefit Adolf Hitler politically?
options: It destroyed evidence of Nazi financial crimes
It allowed him to convince President Hindenburg to suspend civil liberties and arrest communist rivals
It caused the Weimar government to resign immediately
It triggered an open war with France
Hard.hint: Hitler claimed the fire was the signal for a massive, imminent communist revolution.

Hard.q: What immediate impact did the 1848 Public Health Act have on British towns?
options: It instantly cleaned up every major city
It allowed towns to set up health boards, but because it was non-compulsory, many ignored it
It banned the disposal of waste in rivers
It established the first public hospitals
Hard.hint: The lack of enforcement power meant it was highly ineffective until the 1875 Act.

Challenger.q: What specific terms did the 1972 Strategic Arms Limitation Treaty (SALT I) agree upon?
options: A complete ban on all nuclear weapons worldwide
A temporary freeze on the number of strategic ballistic missile launchers and a cap on ABM systems
The withdrawal of all US troops from Europe
The elimination of intermediate-range nuclear forces
Challenger.hint: It was a landmark treaty signed by Richard Nixon and Leonid Brezhnev.

Challenger.q: What was the underlying economic goal of the Nazi policy of 'Autarky' in the 1930s?
options: To increase global free trade with America
To achieve complete economic self-sufficiency so Germany wouldn't rely on imports during wartime
To transition the nation into a pure agricultural society
To hand all factories over to workers' unions
Challenger.hint: It focused heavily on developing synthetic substitutes for oil and rubber.

Challenger.q: How did the 19th-century discovery of chloroform by James Simpson alter the development of surgery?
options: It reduced infection rates in open wounds
It acted as an effective general anesthetic, allowing deeper, more complex operations, though death rates initially rose
Challenger.hint: Because patients couldn't feel pain, surgeons took their time, which ironically caused more deep-tissue infection until antiseptics arrived.

Challenger.q: What major diplomatic shift occurred via the 1972 Basic Treaty between East and West Germany?
options: They merged into a single country
They formally recognized each other as sovereign states for the very first time
They went to war over Berlin borders
They both voted to leave the United Nations
Challenger.hint: It was a vital cornerstone of Willy Brandt's 'Ostpolitik' normalization strategy.

Challenger.q: What mechanism did the 1923 Dawes Plan implement to resolve the hyperinflation crisis in Germany?
options: It completely canceled all German war debts permanently
It lowered immediate reparations payments, arranged massive American loans, and led to the French exit from the Ruhr
It banned the use of foreign money in Germany
It forced Germany to return to the gold standard
Challenger.hint: It tied the Weimar economy directly to American financial prosperity.

Challenger.q: In medieval medicine, what did the treatment of 'trepanning' involve?
options: Bleeding a patient using leeches to adjust biles
Drilling a hole directly into the skull to release evil spirits
Boiling herbs to create an inhaled steam solution
Isolating infected patients outside city walls
Challenger.hint: It was used as an extreme remedy for severe headaches or mental illness.

Challenger.q: What was the purpose of the Cominform organization established by Joseph Stalin in 1947?
options: To coordinate financial aid for rebuilding Russia
To coordinate and control the activities of communist parties across Europe
To manage Soviet nuclear weapons development
To negotiate peace treaties with the USA
Challenger.hint: It ensured all satellite states strictly followed political orders from Moscow.

Challenger.q: What was the role of the Reich Chamber of Culture set up by Joseph Goebbels in 1933?
options: To provide free theater tickets to poor families
To ensure all artists, writers, and musicians were registered and their work promoted Nazi ideals
To teach foreign languages to German diplomats
Challenger.hint: Anyone who was not a member was banned from creating, publishing, or performing art.

Challenger.q: Which medieval Islamic physician wrote 'The Canon of Medicine', an encyclopedic textbook that dominated European medicine for centuries?
Challenger.hint: Known in the West as Avicenna, his medical compilation was highly structured.

Challenger.q: What was the structural impact of the 1961 Berlin Crisis on the global superpower balance?
options: It triggered a nuclear exchange in Europe
It defused the Berlin issue by physically cementing the division, shifting Cold War competition to other continents
It led to the immediate reunification of Germany
It forced the USA to pull out of West Berlin
Challenger.hint: Though it looked tense, the wall actually reduced direct conflict in Europe by stabilizing the border.

Challenger.q: What did the 1933 Concordat agreement between Adolf Hitler and the Catholic Church stipulate?
options: The Catholic Church openly joined the Nazi Party
Hitler promised to protect Catholic institutions and schools if the Church stayed completely out of German politics
The Pope excommunicated the entire German government
Catholic bishops took over the public education system
Challenger.hint: Hitler later broke this agreement, actively targeting Catholic youth groups.

Challenger.q: What did Thomas Sydenham advocate for in 17th-century medical practice that challenged old traditions?
options: Relying strictly on the textbooks of Galen
Observing patients closely at the bedside and tracking their specific symptoms to classify diseases
Using astrology to predict when epidemics would strike
Banning the use of chemical remedies
Challenger.hint: Known as the 'English Hippocrates', he stressed real-world clinical observation over ancient theories.

Challenger.q: What development occurred during the 1960 U-2 Incident?
options: A US nuclear submarine collided with a Soviet ship
A Soviet surface-to-air missile shot down an American spy plane over Russia, collapsing a key peace summit
An American spy ring was caught in East Berlin
The USSR successfully landed a rover on the moon
Challenger.hint: The pilot, Gary Powers, was captured alive, exposing US spying operations.

Challenger.q: What was the function of the Nazi 'Strength Through Joy' (KdF) organization?
options: To provide mandatory military training to workers
To organize affordable leisure activities, holidays, and rewards for workers to keep them loyal
To run public fitness boot camps
To manage the rationing of food items
Challenger.hint: It kept workers happy while independent trade unions were banned.

Challenger.q: Which report published in 1942 laid the foundation for the British Welfare State and the creation of the NHS?
Challenger.hint: It identified five 'Giant Evils' in society: Want, Disease, Ignorance, Squalor, and Idleness.
